text: The video shows the gastrojejunal anastomosis with a visible ulcer, accompanied by minimal spillage of gastric contents.

The surgeon pulls up the omentum and checks its reach for a tension-free patch. The ulcer is closed, and a permanent suture is placed proximal to the ulcer, threading through the omentum. As the knot is tied, the omentum covers the ulcer, forming a laparoscopic omental patch without needing an assistant.
An endoscopic biopsy of the gastrojejunal ulcer is performed through the mouth, using standard endoscopic techniques to obtain tissue samples from the ulcer site.

The video shows a laparoscopic view of the second patient who previously underwent an anticholic-antigastric gastric bypass. The surgical field reveals significant inflammation surrounding the perforated pre-pyloric ulcer, as identified by CT scan.
The surgeon ensures the anacolic rulem is positioned away from the surgical field. The ulcer is identified, and the remnant stomach is accessed for endoscopic evaluation and biopsy.

The surgeon identifies the ulcer and secures the gastric wall using Keith needles. Electrocautery is employed to create a gastrostomy, followed by the insertion of a twelve millimeter trocar into the stomach. A diagnostic endoscope is introduced for endoscopic evaluation and biopsy of the ulcer in the remnant stomach.
The surgeon closes the gastrostomy using a surgical stapler. Subsequently, an omental patch is applied to repair the ulcer, covering the area with omental tissue.

The video shows the small bowel insufflated, which complicates visualization for the remainder of the surgical procedure. A bowel clamp was not applied, contributing to the difficulty in maintaining a clear view.
The retrocholic rulem is visible, with the stomach secured using stay sutures against the anterior abdominal wall, facilitated by the retrocholic positioning.

The surgeon creates a gastrostomy using electrocautery and accesses the stomach with a twelve millimeter trocar. A bowel clamp is applied to the biliopancreatic limb near the jejunojejunostomy to prevent insufflation of the small bowel during endoscopy. An endoscopic examination of the remnant stomach is performed, and biopsies are taken from the ulcer site.
The surgeon uses a stapler to close the gastrostomy, followed by the application of an omental patch to repair the ulcer.
